FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes baseline ABCD Study data (N=9,718; Data release 2.0.1); U.S. multisite longitudinal study of children ages 9-11.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using machine learning framework (stacked ensemble of elastic net, SVR, CIF, kNN) with nested cross-validation, plus follow-up LME analyses, applied to ABCD baseline data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in Child Development (2021); no indication of duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Follow-up LME analyses report separate F-statistics for specific phone/SM variables paired with fluid cognitive functioning (FCF, individual-trait cognitive outcome): social media use F(1,9664)=6.79, p<.01; texting F(1,9669)=7.99, p<.005; video chatting F(1,9666)=6.81, p<.01. Also, Table 2 reports Spearman r=-0.148 (p<.001) for weekday screen media activity (STQ) with FCF. Social media and texting are qualifying phone/SM variables; FCF is individual-trait cognitive outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: Spearman r values in Table 2, variable importance (VI) scores from stacked ML model, F-statistics and p-values from LME follow-up analyses.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analyses conducted: ML model with permutation feature importance and stacked ensemble, plus LME models with F-statistics for screen media activity subtypes predicting FCF.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional (authors explicitly state: "The results presented here are cross-sectional"). EC7 requires longitudinal design; cross-sectional carve-out also applies since SM is DV in no analysis -- SM is IV/predictor throughout. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Screen media activity is an IV (predictor) of fluid cognitive functioning (DV). Cross-sectional ABCD baseline data. STQ measures social network sites, texting, video chat separately, all reported with individual estimates in LME follow-up analyses.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes ABCD Study prospective cohort data (N=11,025); ABCD 3.0 release; 21 U.S. sites; ages 9-10 at baseline.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis; logistic regression of baseline screen time modalities predicting binge-eating disorder at 1-year follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in International Journal of Eating Disorders (2021); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 2 reports aOR 1.62 (95% CI 1.18-2.22, p=.003) for social networking predicting binge-eating disorder; aOR 1.40 (95% CI 1.08-1.82, p=.011) for texting; aOR 1.32 (95% CI 0.99-1.76, p=.057) for video chat. Social networking and texting are qualifying phone/SM variables (IV); binge-eating disorder (DSM-5 diagnosis via KSADS-5) is an individual-trait health outcome (DV).

FT_IC3.evidence: Unadjusted and adjusted ORs with 95% CIs and p-values reported in Table 2 for each screen time modality predicting binge-eating disorder at 1-year follow-up.

FT_IC4.evidence: Logistic regression analyses with reported aOR, 95% CI, and p-values constitute inferential analyses with numeric results.

FT_EC7_reason: SM/phone variables are IVs (exposures) predicting binge-eating disorder as DV. EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis; here SM is IV throughout. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Prospective longitudinal design (baseline screen time predicting 1-year follow-up binge-eating disorder). Screen time modalities include social networking, texting, video chat, video games, TV/movies, YouTube -- each reported separately. ABCD 3.0 release.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes ABCD Study prospective cohort data (N=11,066); ABCD 3.0 release; 21 U.S. sites; ages 9-10 at baseline.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis; multiple linear regression of baseline screen time modalities predicting BMI percentile at 1-year follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in Pediatric Obesity (2021); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 2 reports B=0.92 (95% CI 0.29-1.55, p=.004) for texting predicting BMI percentile at 1-year follow-up (fully adjusted); B=0.72 (95% CI 0.09-1.36, p=.025) for video chat; B=0.67 (95% CI -0.07-1.41, p=.075) for social networking. Texting and video chat are qualifying phone/SM variables (IV); BMI percentile is an individual-trait physical health outcome (DV).

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression coefficients (B) with 95% CIs and p-values reported in Table 2 for each screen time modality predicting BMI percentile at 1-year follow-up.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses with B coefficients, 95% CIs, and p-values constitute inferential analyses with numeric results.

FT_EC7_reason: SM/phone variables (texting, video chat, social networking) are IVs predicting BMI percentile as DV. EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis; here SM is IV throughout. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Prospective longitudinal design (baseline screen time predicting 1-year follow-up BMI percentile). Screen time modalities include texting, video chat, social networking, video games, TV/movies, YouTube -- each reported separately in 14 separate regression models. ABCD 3.0 release. Closely related companion paper to paper_id 24 (same cohort, same baseline, same first author, same year, different outcomes).
